description: Most-used utility classes: UGameplayStatics, UKismetSystemLibrary, UKismetMathLibrary, FMath, and common helper functions

entries.title: UGameplayStatics — The Most Used Utility Class
entries.description: UGameplayStatics is a static Blueprint function library providing quick access to the most common gameplay operations: spawning effects, playing sounds, getting players, applying damage, and more.
entries.whatItIs: UGameplayStatics is a static-only UBlueprintFunctionLibrary that serves as a grab-bag of the most frequently needed gameplay operations. It is the single most-called utility class in typical UE5 projects, accessible from both C++ and Blueprints without needing an instance.
whenToUse: When you need quick access to the player character, controller, game mode, or game state from any context
When applying damage through the UE5 damage framework (ApplyDamage, ApplyRadialDamage, ApplyPointDamage)
When playing sounds or spawning particle effects at world locations
When loading a new level or querying the current level name
When you need a one-off gameplay query like GetAllActorsOfClass or PredictProjectilePath
When setting global time dilation for slow-motion effects
whenNotToUse: When you already have a direct reference to the object you need — calling GetPlayerCharacter every frame is wasteful if you can cache the pointer
For GetAllActorsOfClass in Tick — this is extremely expensive and results should be cached or replaced with TActorIterator or tagging systems
When you need Niagara particle systems — use UNiagaraFunctionLibrary instead of the legacy Cascade SpawnEmitter functions
When you need multiplayer-aware player access — GetPlayerCharacter(World, 0) only gets player index 0 and is a single-player shorthand
commonMistakes: Calling GetAllActorsOfClass every frame instead of caching the result or using event-driven lookup
Using GetPlayerCharacter(World, 0) in multiplayer — this only returns the local first player, not the replicated character
Forgetting that OpenLevel is a hard load that destroys all current actors — use seamless travel for multiplayer
Using SpawnEmitterAtLocation for Niagara systems — it only works with legacy Cascade particle systems
Setting GlobalTimeDilation to 0 which freezes everything including your own unpause logic
keyPoints: GetPlayerCharacter(World, 0): get the first player's character (single-player shorthand)
GetPlayerController(World, 0): get the first player controller
GetPlayerCameraManager(World, 0): get the camera manager
GetGameMode(), GetGameState(), GetGameInstance(): quick accessors
ApplyDamage(Actor, Damage, Instigator, Causer, DamageType): applies damage through the damage framework
ApplyPointDamage() / ApplyRadialDamage(): directional and area damage variants
PlaySoundAtLocation(World, Sound, Location, Rotation, VolumeMultiplier, PitchMultiplier)
PlaySound2D(World, Sound): plays non-spatialized audio
SpawnEmitterAtLocation(): spawn a particle system at a point
SpawnEmitterAttached(): spawn particles attached to a component
OpenLevel(World, LevelName) / OpenLevelBySoftObjectPtr(): change maps
GetCurrentLevelName(): get the current map name
SetGlobalTimeDilation(World, Dilation): slow-mo or speed-up (1.0 = normal)
GetAllActorsOfClass(World, Class, OutActors): find all actors of a type (expensive — cache results)
PredictProjectilePath(): simulates a projectile arc for trajectory preview

entries.title: UKismetSystemLibrary — System Utilities
entries.description: UKismetSystemLibrary provides system-level utilities: debug drawing, traces with debug visualization, print strings, platform info, and more.
entries.whatItIs: UKismetSystemLibrary is a static Blueprint function library focused on system-level operations and debugging tools. It wraps trace functions with built-in debug visualization, provides PrintString for on-screen/log output, and includes platform utility queries.
whenToUse: When you need trace functions (line, sphere, box, capsule) with built-in debug drawing for rapid prototyping
When using PrintString for quick on-screen debug output during development
When you need to draw debug shapes (lines, spheres, boxes, arrows) to visualize game logic
When checking if a UObject is valid and not pending kill via IsValid
When checking if an actor implements an interface at runtime via DoesImplementInterface
When you need to quit the application programmatically
whenNotToUse: In shipping/release builds for debug drawing — wrap calls in #if !UE_BUILD_SHIPPING or use DrawDebugType::None
For high-performance traces in Tick — use the raw FCollisionQueryParams-based World->LineTraceSingleByChannel directly to avoid overhead
For permanent in-game visual lines or shapes — use procedural mesh, spline mesh, or decals instead of debug draw
For user-facing log output — PrintString is for developers; use UMG/Slate widgets for player-facing text
commonMistakes: Leaving PrintString and DrawDebug calls in shipping builds — they add overhead and look unprofessional
Using UKismetSystemLibrary traces in performance-critical code when raw UWorld traces would be faster
Confusing IsValid(Object) with a simple null check — IsValid also checks PendingKill state which a raw pointer check does not
Passing EDrawDebugTrace::ForDuration with a 0 duration, causing debug lines to disappear immediately
Forgetting to populate the ActorsToIgnore array when self-hits are causing false positives
keyPoints: PrintString(World, String, bPrintToScreen, bPrintToLog, Color, Duration): debug output
DrawDebugLine, DrawDebugSphere, DrawDebugBox, DrawDebugArrow: visual debug shapes
LineTraceSingle (with DrawDebugType): line trace WITH optional debug visualization built-in
SphereTraceSingle, BoxTraceSingle, CapsuleTraceSingle: sweep traces with debug drawing
LineTraceMulti: returns all hits, not just the first
IsValid(Object): checks if UObject reference is valid and not pending kill
DoesImplementInterface(Object, InterfaceClass): check if an actor implements an interface
GetDisplayName(Object): human-readable name for debug output
SetTimer, ClearTimer (Blueprint versions): timer utilities
QuitGame(): exits the application

entries.title: FMath — Math Utilities
entries.description: FMath is UE5's math utility class providing clamping, interpolation, random numbers, angle operations, and common math functions used constantly in gameplay code.
entries.whatItIs: FMath is a core engine struct containing static math helper functions used in virtually every UE5 project. It provides framerate-independent interpolation, clamping, random number generation, trigonometry, and float comparison utilities that form the backbone of gameplay math.
whenToUse: When clamping values to a valid range (health, speed, UI percentages)
When smoothly interpolating values over time — FInterpTo, VInterpTo, and RInterpTo are framerate-independent and ideal for Tick
When generating random numbers with FMath::RandRange for gameplay variation
When comparing floating-point values where exact equality is unreliable — use IsNearlyEqual or IsNearlyZero
When remapping a value from one range to another with GetMappedRangeValueClamped
When converting between degrees and radians for trigonometric calculations
whenNotToUse: When UKismetMathLibrary already provides a more convenient Blueprint-friendly wrapper for the same operation
For complex curve-based interpolation — use UCurveFloat or FRuntimeFloatCurve instead of manual Lerp
For deterministic multiplayer random — FMath::RandRange uses a global RNG; use FRandomStream with a shared seed instead
For SIMD-optimized batch math on large data sets — consider VectorRegister intrinsics or UE5's ISPC support
commonMistakes: Using FMath::Lerp in Tick without DeltaTime — this gives framerate-dependent results; use FInterpTo instead
Passing an Alpha > 1 or < 0 to Lerp and expecting it to clamp — Lerp extrapolates; use FMath::Clamp on the alpha
Using FMath::RandRange in networked games without a synchronized seed, causing desync between client and server
Comparing floats with == instead of FMath::IsNearlyEqual, leading to subtle bugs from floating-point imprecision
Forgetting that FMath::Sin/Cos take radians, not degrees — use FMath::DegreesToRadians or UKismetMathLibrary::DegSin
keyPoints: FMath::Clamp(Value, Min, Max): restrict a value to a range. Use everywhere.
FMath::Lerp(A, B, Alpha): linear interpolation. Alpha 0=A, 1=B. Core of smooth movement.
FMath::FInterpTo(Current, Target, DeltaTime, Speed): smooth interpolation over time (framerate-independent)
FMath::VInterpTo(): same but for FVector. RInterpTo() for FRotator.
FMath::RandRange(Min, Max): random int or float in range (inclusive)
FMath::Abs(Value): absolute value
FMath::FloorToInt(), CeilToInt(), RoundToInt(): float to int conversions
FMath::DegreesToRadians(), RadiansToDegrees(): angle conversion
FMath::Sin(), Cos(), Atan2(): trig functions (radians)
FMath::IsNearlyEqual(A, B, Tolerance): float comparison with epsilon
FMath::IsNearlyZero(Value, Tolerance): near-zero check
FMath::GetMappedRangeValueClamped(): remap a value from one range to another

entries.title: FVector, FRotator, FTransform — Core Math Types
entries.description: The three most used spatial math types in UE5. FVector for positions/directions, FRotator for orientations, FTransform for the full position+rotation+scale.
entries.whatItIs: FVector, FRotator, and FTransform are the fundamental spatial math types in UE5. FVector represents 3D positions, directions, and velocities; FRotator stores orientation as Pitch/Yaw/Roll in degrees; and FTransform combines location, rotation (as FQuat), and scale into a single struct used throughout the engine's transform hierarchy.
whenToUse: FVector: whenever you need to represent a 3D position, direction, velocity, or offset
FRotator: when working with human-readable orientation in degrees (camera angles, actor facing, aim direction)
FTransform: when you need to store or pass a complete position + rotation + scale, such as spawn transforms or socket transforms
FQuat: when performing complex rotation composition, interpolation (Slerp), or when you need to avoid gimbal lock
whenNotToUse: FRotator for composing multiple rotations — use FQuat to avoid gimbal lock and numerical drift
FVector::Size() in tight loops for distance comparisons — use SizeSquared() or DistSquared() to avoid the square root
FTransform when you only need position — passing a full transform is heavier than passing an FVector
Raw FVector math for complex skeletal transforms — use the bone transform API instead which handles spaces correctly
commonMistakes: Using Normalize() on a zero-length vector which produces NaN — always use GetSafeNormal() which returns ZeroVector for zero-length input
Confusing FRotator component order — it is Pitch (Y), Yaw (Z), Roll (X), NOT X/Y/Z
Using FVector::Dist() in performance-critical loops — FVector::DistSquared() avoids the expensive square root and works fine for comparisons
Chaining multiple FRotator additions which can accumulate gimbal lock — convert to FQuat, compose, then convert back
Forgetting that FRotator stores degrees while FMath trig functions expect radians
keyPoints: FVector(X, Y, Z): position, direction, or velocity. FVector::ZeroVector, ForwardVector, UpVector.
FVector operations: +, -, *, Size(), SizeSquared(), Normalize(), GetSafeNormal(), Dot, Cross
FVector::Dist(A, B): distance between two points. DistSquared() for cheaper comparison.
FRotator(Pitch, Yaw, Roll): orientation in degrees. FRotator::ZeroRotator.
FRotator::MakeFromX(Direction): create rotation from a forward direction vector
FQuat: quaternion rotation. More stable than FRotator for complex rotations. FQuat::Slerp() for smooth rotation.
FTransform: combines Location (FVector) + Rotation (FQuat) + Scale (FVector).
GetActorTransform(), SetActorTransform(): full transform operations
.RotateVector(V): rotate a direction by the rotator/quat
FMath::FindLookAtRotation(From, To): rotation that points from one location to another

entries.title: Cast<T>, IsValid, IsA — Type Safety Functions
entries.description: The most commonly used type-checking and safety functions in UE5 C++. Use these constantly to avoid crashes from invalid pointers and wrong types.
entries.whatItIs: Cast<T>, IsValid, and IsA are the core type-safety primitives in UE5 C++. They leverage UE5's UObject reflection system to perform safe runtime type checks and conversions, replacing raw C++ static_cast/dynamic_cast with engine-aware alternatives that understand the UClass hierarchy.
whenToUse: Cast<T>: whenever you receive a base-class pointer (like AActor*) and need to access a subclass's members
IsValid: before dereferencing any UObject pointer, especially ones received from delegates, timers, or async callbacks
IsA<T>: when you only need to check an object's type without accessing subclass-specific members
CastChecked<T>: in places where the cast must succeed and failure indicates a logic bug (crashes in debug to catch errors early)
check/ensure: for asserting invariants and preconditions in your code
whenNotToUse: Cast<T> on non-UObject types — it only works with UObject-derived classes; use static_cast or dynamic_cast for plain C++ classes
CastChecked<T> on user-provided or external input that could legitimately be the wrong type — it will crash in debug builds
check() in code paths that can legitimately fail at runtime — use if-statements and error handling instead
IsValid in tight inner loops on pointers you already know are valid — the PendingKill check adds minor overhead
commonMistakes: Using static_cast instead of Cast<T> for UObject types — static_cast skips the type check and will silently produce a bad pointer if the type is wrong
Checking if(Ptr) instead of IsValid(Ptr) for UObjects — a raw null check misses the PendingKill state, leading to access on destroyed objects
Not checking the return value of Cast<T> — always check for nullptr before using the result
Using CastChecked<T> in production code paths where failure is possible — this crashes the game instead of handling the error gracefully
Forgetting that ensure() only fires once per callsite — subsequent failures at the same line are silently ignored
keyPoints: Cast<T>(Object): safe downcast for UObject types. Returns nullptr if the cast fails. Always use over static_cast.
IsValid(Object): checks non-null AND not pending destruction. Use before any UObject pointer access.
IsA<T>(): checks if an object is of type T (or a subclass) without casting
CastChecked<T>(Object): Cast that asserts on failure — crashes in debug builds. Use when the cast MUST succeed.
ExactCast<T>(): cast that only succeeds if the object is exactly type T, not a subclass
ensure(Expression): like check() but only fires in debug builds and doesn't crash in shipping
check(Expression): assertion that crashes if false. Use for logic that must never fail.
checkf(Expression, Format, ...): check with a formatted error message
TObjectPtr<T>: UE5 replacement for raw UObject pointers in UPROPERTY. Adds access tracking.
::IsValid() vs if(Ptr): IsValid also checks PendingKill. Always prefer IsValid for UObjects.

entries.title: UKismetMathLibrary — Blueprint Math Functions
entries.description: UKismetMathLibrary exposes math functions to Blueprints but is also usable from C++. Contains vector math, rotations, random functions, and convenience math.
entries.whatItIs: UKismetMathLibrary is a static Blueprint function library that mirrors and extends FMath operations for Blueprint accessibility. It provides degree-based trigonometry, vector/rotator convenience functions, range mapping, and random generation utilities that are callable from both Blueprints and C++.
whenToUse: In Blueprints for any math operation — this is the primary math library exposed to the Blueprint graph
When you need degree-based trig functions (DegSin, DegCos, DegAtan2) without manual conversion
For FindLookAtRotation and GetDirectionUnitVector which are cleaner than manual vector subtraction and normalization
When remapping values between ranges with MapRangeClamped — commonly used for UI progress bars and gameplay scaling
For generating random points or directions for scatter, spread, or spawn patterns
whenNotToUse: In performance-critical C++ code where FMath equivalents exist — FMath avoids the UFunction dispatch overhead
When you need deterministic random results in multiplayer — use FRandomStream with a synchronized seed instead
For complex matrix math or transform composition — use FMatrix or FTransform directly
When the same operation is already available as an FVector/FRotator member function — calling the member directly is cleaner in C++
commonMistakes: Using UKismetMathLibrary in hot C++ loops instead of FMath — the Kismet wrapper has overhead from UFunction infrastructure
Confusing MapRangeClamped (clamps output) with MapRangeUnclamped (allows extrapolation) and getting unexpected values
Using RandomUnitVector for 2D gameplay and getting unexpected Z-axis variation — project onto a plane or use FMath::RandRange for angle instead
Calling FindLookAtRotation with identical Start and Target points, producing an undefined rotation
Forgetting that NormalizeToRange returns 0-1 only if the input is within Min-Max — values outside the range extrapolate
keyPoints: FindLookAtRotation(Start, Target): rotation from one point facing another
GetDirectionUnitVector(From, To): normalized direction between two points
ProjectVectorOnToVector(): project one vector onto another
RandomUnitVector(): random direction (great for spread/scatter patterns)
RandomPointInBoundingBox(): random point inside a box
MakeRotFromX/Y/Z(): make rotation from a single axis direction
NormalizeToRange(Value, Min, Max): remap to 0-1
MapRangeClamped(Value, InMin, InMax, OutMin, OutMax): remap between ranges
InverseLerp(A, B, Value): find alpha that would produce Value from Lerp(A, B, Alpha)
DegSin, DegCos, DegAtan2: trig functions in degrees (not radians)
